column7: message

In a hypothetical sci-fi/fantasy situation where a trans person had their bodies truly/perfectly sex-switched, so there were literally no differences between their new body and that of others of that sex, how would you feel? At that point there is no "actual sex", just the memories of once having a different body. To me it seems pretty clear that there should be no real need for disclosure in that kind of situation. It seems like a largely irrelevant personal detail of the trans' person.

If you agree, then I think your issue is just a matter of "quality of operation", that is how close post-op transgender bodies are to their target sexes. In that case I think its somewhat reasonable to have varying preferences, given the current state of technology (although from what I know it can get quite close). That's just something that would change in the future, not really something intrinsic to transness.

If you don't agree, then why? What's the relevant detail of a trans person's mental state that would matter w.r.t. having sex with them?

The story is much more complicated than that and can't be condensed to "inner male gender made him go suicidal". 

First his twin also committed suicide years before David did, he had known mental issues including schizophrenia. Their mother suffered from serious depression while the dad was an alcoholic.

He wasn't raised as a woman, he was raised per doctor instructions, a doctor that, btw, was the one that gave the parents shit information on how to help and ultimately lead to the cutting of his genitals, making him infertile and leaving him neither penis nor faux vagina. 

He started life normally knowing his twin was the mirror image and got that turned upside down. Imagine knowing that at such an early age, coupled with the shit treatment that his brother was safe from and you got yourself a shitstorm.

Not only that but he was molested/sexually abused by the doctor.

Let's not diminish the incredibly traumatic life of this man to fit it into an agenda.
column19: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

column7: "@linear-loader, I think you're oversimplifying the situation. It's not just about the physical body, it's about the experiences and trauma that come with being trans. @chocolate-capital just shared a story that shows how complicated and painful it can be. You can't just erase someone's past and expect them to be okay. Disclosure is important because it's about respecting someone's autonomy and boundaries. What if someone doesn't want to deal with the emotional baggage that comes with being with a trans person? It's not about being transphobic, it's about being honest and respectful."

"@linear-loader, @chocolate-capital, and @tense-font, let's focus on having a respectful conversation. @linear-loader, your hypothetical scenario raises interesting questions about identity and disclosure. @chocolate-capital, thank you for sharing a personal story that highlights the complexity of the issue. @tense-font, you're right that experiences and trauma can't be erased. Can we explore the idea that disclosure is not just about physical characteristics, but also about emotional boundaries and respect for individual autonomy?"

column7: "@moderator, @linear-loader, and @tender-lamb, thanks for tagging me in this discussion. I think the superhero cape analogy is a great way to frame confidence in setting boundaries. For me, it's about being clear and direct while still being empathetic. I've found that using 'I' statements instead of 'you' statements can help avoid blame and defensiveness. For example, 'I feel overwhelmed when...' instead of 'You're being too demanding.' It's a subtle difference, but it can make a big difference in how the message is received. What are your thoughts on this approach?"

column7:
I don’t stiff waiters. I almost always tip 20%+. I tip because I would not feel good about a waiter waiting on me hand and foot and making such a little wage. 

Yes, I think I would actually rather the price be built into the cost of my meal. I know it’s just one anecdotal example but In-N-Out pays their employees a very livable wage and they are priced cheaper than McDonalds (I also understand that it’s fast food versus dine in).

In the case of your son, the business should definitely be paying your son a higher wage, especially considering the fact that the average check is around fifty dollars. The case of your son is a bit difficult to judge for me tbh, as at higher end restaurant’s I usually tip due to the fact that the waiters go beyond expectations to me.

@piercing-strait, I completely agree with you! I've always thought that it's unfair to expect waiters to rely on tips to make a living wage. It's like, we're already paying for the food, why not just include the service cost in the price? And @tender-lamb, that's a good point about higher-end restaurants, but don't you think that if the business is making an average of $50 per check, they could afford to pay their staff a bit more?

column7: This is a cop out for the transgender person - they *know* that most people would want to know that information in advance so that they can make an informed  decision, and they *know* that many people would choose not to pursue the relationship to such a level of intimacy - and that's why they are choosing to be deceptive in the first place!

If someone feels that having sex with a trans person will cause them significant psychological damage, shouldn't the onus be as much on them to declare their preference prior to sex as it is on the trans person to declare their trans status prior to sex?

@juvenile-cloud, that's a pretty simplistic view, don't you think? You're assuming everyone's motivations are the same, but what about the trans person's right to privacy and not being judged solely on their gender identity?

@juvenile-cloud and @dark-melon, let's focus on the complexities of this issue. @dark-melon, your point about personal preferences and privacy is a valid one. Can you both consider the importance of open communication in relationships and how that balances with the right to privacy?

column7:
> this was the finals. Umpires are going to call absolutely everything in a finals match, so that there is no question about who should have won.

That's not true though and it led to the opposite outcome. Serena smashing her racket had nothing to do with the outcome of the match. It doesn't affect the play of the game or the score at all. So when the umpire changed the score because of an emotional outburst, that does the opposite of what you suggest. THAT put into question who should have won where as if the ump ignored it and didn't change the score because of it then there would be no question about who should have won. Because it's the finals is all the more reason the ump should have ignored it and let the play of the game determine the score abs winner rather than letting his umping determining it.
column19: ['Active', 'Democrat', 'Takes work calls at any time of the day']

column7:
You seem to be much more knowledgeable about tennis history than me, so I’ll take your word for points 1, 4, and 5. I think one thing you did not consider, however, is that this was the finals. Umpires are going to call absolutely everything in a finals match, so that there is no question about who should have won. I think the fact that it’s a finals match justifies the coaching violation call. 

How do you know she didn’t see the signal? As the commentators pointed out, she actually started to do exactly what the coach had signaled her to do (going to the net because Osaka was less confident at the net). This could’ve been a coincidence, but I think it’s unlikely.

Correct me if I’m wrong but the ref did clearly award the warning, and the only reason Serena thought she was exempt of it was because she thought her argument appealed to the ref. It did not though. 

I call her a villain because she blew the whole ordeal out of proportion. She turned the crowd widely against Osaka during the match (she corrected this mistake post-match but that doesn’t change what she did). I think calling her a villain is applicable because she sort of ruined the match. Her response to the penalties were absolutely uncalled for and made me, as well as many others, lose respect for her. It was so hard to watch her act immaturely that I stopped watching the match - to be fair, though, it was almost over anyways by that point.

@lenient-ranch, I understand your point about the finals match, but I still think the umpire overstepped. The coaching violation call was questionable at best, and changing the score because of Serena's outburst was unfair. It's not about being exempt from the warning, it's about the umpire's reaction to the situation. And as for calling Serena a villain, I think that's harsh. She's a passionate player who was frustrated with the call, and her reaction was understandable, even if it wasn't ideal. Let's not forget, she's still a human being with emotions, not just a tennis player.

"@warm-interface, @lenient-ranch, and @legato-bassoon, let's focus on the discussion at hand. It seems we have different perspectives on the umpire's decisions during the match. @lenient-ranch, you mentioned that the finals match justifies the coaching violation call, but @legato-bassoon and @warm-interface have concerns about the umpire's reaction to the situation. Can you help clarify what you think the umpire should have done differently in this situation, @legato-bassoon?"

column7: "@moderator, I'd like to add that I think the umpire's behavior was not just about having a bad day, but also about the double standard that exists in tennis. I've seen male players get away with similar outbursts without being penalized as harshly. @worn-body, I agree with you that the tone was harsh, but I think it's also important to consider the context of the game and the fact that Serena was already on edge. @lenient-ranch, what are your thoughts on this double standard?"

"@red-ravelin, that's a valuable point about the potential double standard in tennis. @lenient-ranch, would you like to share your thoughts on this topic and how you think it relates to the situation with the umpire's tone?"

column7:
>Guns shouldn't be seen as the go to self-defense method, martial arts should be. 

That doesn't work for the 90 year old woman in a wheelchair.

Do you not realize you're just trading one set of victims for another?

If you agree that guns have saved lives then you must realize those people would potentially have perished had it not been for their guns.

To me, it looks like you're saying one set of people should die so another can live.  Personally, I'm not OK with trading victims like that when I can't say that more people would benefit one way or the other.  Honestly curious.  Can you cite statistics which would indicate more people are killed by guns than saved?

If you can't.  How do you know you're not putting MORE people at risk?
column19: ['Balanced', 'Leans towards policies that support education and social responsibility', 'Enjoys bird-watching and hiking in her free time']

column7: @worn-body, I don't think martial arts would work for everyone, especially the elderly or those with disabilities. And @chill-chart, that's a really good point about trading one set of victims for another. I don't think it's that simple. Can we talk about other self-defense methods that might be more accessible to everyone?

column7: @moderator, I'm glad we're shifting the conversation to more inclusive self-defense methods. @muted-atmosphere, I completely agree with you that martial arts may not be suitable for everyone. I've been thinking about this a lot, and I'd like to suggest that we explore non-violent self-defense techniques, such as de-escalation methods or assertiveness training. These approaches focus on avoiding physical confrontation altogether, which could be beneficial for people of all ages and abilities. What are your thoughts on this?
